serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of Japchae weighs approximately 350 g, providing around 550 kcal, and is typically served as a main course or shared side dish in a Korean meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Korean Japchae (glass noodles with vegetables and beef), composite estimate from SR Legacy ingredient data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Japchae as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy ingredient data for sweet potato starch noodles (dangmyeon), lean beef, spinach, carrots, shiitake mushrooms, soy sauce, and sesame oil. Values are retained from the input where plausible and consistent with USDA ingredient-level data. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients not reported in the input are set to -1 (data not available at composite level).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — sweet potato starch noodles (dangmyeon) analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Japchae as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is estimated based on the primary carbohydrate source: dangmyeon (sweet potato starch glass noodles), which has a published GI of approximately 45 (low GI) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The vegetable and protein components further moderate postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 350 g serving calculated as: (45 × 55.0 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 24.8 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 342.86 mg per 100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,200 mg sodium, approaching 52% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce in preparation to bring sodium below 250 mg per 100 g, avoid adding condiments at the table, and limit portion size to 200 g to keep per-meal sodium under 700 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 15.71 g per 100 g with GI approximately 45 (low); a 350 g serving provides approximately 55 g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~24.8, which is moderate-to-high for a single meal.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit Japchae to a 150–200 g portion to keep glycemic load below 15, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and request reduced marinade sugar in the bulgogi to minimise added-sugar contribution.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.0 g per 100 g from bulgogi beef; sodium 342.86 mg per 100 g; trans fat 0.14 g per 100 g from beef; sesame oil provides cardioprotective MUFA.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should substitute bulgogi beef with lean chicken breast or tofu to reduce saturated fat and trans fat, retain sesame oil for its MUFA content, and request low-sodium soy sauce to reduce per-serving sodium below 600 mg.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Bulgogi beef is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) that amplifies inflammatory pathways via TNF-alpha and IL-6; bell peppers are nightshade vegetables that may trigger inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute bulgogi beef with tofu, shiitake mushrooms, or chicken to eliminate the arachidonic acid source, monitor individual tolerance to nightshade vegetables such as bell peppers, and retain sesame oil and spinach for their anti-inflammatory properties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Japchae (잡채) is a classic Korean stir-fried glass noodle dish considered one of Korea's most beloved and festive foods, traditionally served at celebrations, holidays, and royal banquets. The dish is built around dangmyeon — translucent, chewy noodles made from sweet potato starch — which are boiled, then stir-fried in sesame oil and seasoned with soy sauce and a small amount of sugar. The noodles are combined with bulgogi-style marinated beef (thinly sliced sirloin or ribeye), julienned carrots, wilted spinach, sliced shiitake or oyster mushrooms, onion, and egg strips (jidan), each ingredient typically seasoned and cooked separately before being tossed together. The flavour profile is savoury, slightly sweet, and nutty from sesame oil, with a satisfying chewy-tender texture contrast between the glass noodles and the crisp-tender vegetables. Nutritionally, Japchae provides approximately 157 kcal per 100 g, with 8 g protein, 7.1 g fat, and 15.7 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low GI) based on the sweet potato starch noodle base, making it a lower-GI alternative to wheat-based noodle dishes. Sodium at 342.86 mg per 100 g is a moderate concern for hypertension management. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans in its standard form due to beef and egg, and contains soy and sesame allergens. Clinically, portion control is important for Type 2 Diabetes management due to the moderate glycemic load of a full serving.

These values require laboratory analysis of a standardised recipe. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.14 g per 100 g, reflecting naturally occurring trans fat from ruminant beef (vaccenic acid / conjugated linoleic acid), not industrial partially hydrogenated oil. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on dangmyeon (sweet potato starch glass noodles) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. 4. The input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore and ethnic_tags including Bibimbap and Korean Fried Chicken — these have been corrected: Japchae originates from Korea; ethnic_tags have been rationalised to Korean cuisine only. 5.
